perturbation.original_text: Following the expiration or early termination of this Agreement, Metavante shall provide to Customer the Customer Data in the format in which it exists on Metavante's systems (the "Data Tapes"), in accordance with Metavante's then-current standard prices for the delivery media.
perturbation.changed_text: Following the expiration or early termination of this Agreement, Metavante may, at its discretion, provide the Customer Data.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the obligation of Metavante to provide the Customer Data. Federal regulations require financial institutions to have access to their data, and this change allows the service provider to withhold it.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 12 CFR § 341.14 - Access to bank information and examination
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 12 CFR § 341.14, which requires that a bank's board of directors and examiners have access to the bank's information and the ability to examine the activities of a service provider. By making the provision of data discretionary, it impedes the bank's ability to comply with these regulatory requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a mandatory obligation for Metavante to provide customer data with a discretionary option, potentially preventing the bank from accessing its own information. The Law Explanation states that this change contradicts 12 CFR § 341.14, because it impedes the bank's ability to comply with regulatory requirements that ensure access to the bank’s information. The perturbation explanation supports this by stating that this change removes the obligation of Metavante to provide the Customer Data, potentially violating federal regulations that require financial institutions to have access to their data. Because the original text had an obligation and the changed text makes it discretionary, this is an omission contradiction. Scraped law snippet 1 shows difficulty in accessing the Federal Register and eCFR.gov sites which could contain 12 CFR § 341.14 mentioned by the LLM.

perturbation.original_text: Customer acknowledges that Metavante's execution of the Form 4804 Affidavit or the equivalent form with the Department on Customer's behalf does not relieve Customer of responsibility to provide accurate TINs or liability for any penalties which may be assessed for failure to comply with TIN requirements.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer acknowledges that Metavante's execution of the Form 4804 Affidavit or the equivalent form with the Department on Customer's behalf may relieve Customer of responsibility to provide accurate TINs or liability for any penalties which may be assessed for failure to comply with TIN requirements.
perturbation.explanation: This modification provides an opening that Metavante's actions could relieve the customer of responsibility to provide accurate TINs, contradicting IRS regulations that mandate accurate TINs. This creates a legal contradiction with IRS regulations about Taxpayer Identification Numbers.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts 26 U.S. Code § 6109, which mandates the use of taxpayer identification numbers (TINs) for tax purposes. The IRS uses TINs to track individuals and businesses for tax compliance. By creating the possibility that Metavante's action relieves the Customer of this responsibility, it could facilitate non-compliance with IRS regulations. The law requires taxpayers to furnish their TIN so returns or statements can be properly processed by the IRS.
perturbation.location: First paragraph, after Exhibit A list.

Identifying numbers (a) Supplying of identifying numbers When required by regulations prescribed by the Secretary: (1) Inclusion in returns Any person required under the authority of this title to make a return, statement, or other document shall include in such return, statement, or other document such identifying number as
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'does not relieve' to 'may relieve,' creating a potential loophole where the customer is no longer responsible for providing accurate TINs. This directly contradicts 26 U.S. Code § 6109, which, as stated in the Law Explanation, mandates the use of taxpayer identification numbers (TINs) for tax purposes, and requires taxpayers to furnish their TIN. Scraped Law Snippet 1 also supports this by stating, 'Any person required under the authority of this title to make a return, statement, or other document shall include in such return, statement, or other document such identifying number as'. The change weakens the legal requirement for customers to provide accurate TINs and comply with IRS regulations.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, Metavante's maximum liability with respect to any Professional Services performed shall be limited to the value of the Professional Services engagement giving rise to the claim for Damages.
perturbation.changed_text: Metavante's liability with respect to any Professional Services performed shall be determined at Metavante's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the clause to 'determined at Metavante's sole discretion,' we remove any set liability, potentially leaving consumers without a recourse. This is a legal contradiction, because it violates consumer protection laws, specifically laws regarding liability for damages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 2304 - Remedies under written warranty or service contract; fulfillment of warranty
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 2304, which details the responsibilities a warrantor has to remedy a product within a reasonable time. Allowing Metavante to determine liability at its 'sole discretion' could result in them not providing any sort of remedy, violating this law.

Federal minimum standards for warranties (a) Remedies under written warranty; duration of implied warranty; exclusion or limitation on consequential damages for breach of written or implied warranty; election of refund or replacement In order for a warrantor warranting a consumer product by means of a written warranty to meet the Federal minimum standards for warranty- (1) such warrantor must as a minimum remedy such consumer product within a reasonable time and without char
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 16 CFR § 701.3 - Written warranty terms. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 16 CFR § 701.3 - Written warranty terms. CFR prev | next § 701.3 Written warranty terms. (a) Any warrantor warranting to a consumer by means of a written warranty a consumer product actually costing the consumer more than $15.00 shall clearly and conspicuously disclose in a single document in simple and readily understood language, the following items of information: (1) The identity of the party or parties to whom the written warranty is extended, if the enforceability of the written warranty is limited to the original consumer purchaser or is otherwise limited to persons other than every consumer owner during the term of the warranty; (2) A clear description and identification of products, or parts, or characteristics, or components or properties covered by and where necessary for clarification, excluded from the warranty; (3) A statement of what the warrantor will do in the event of a defect, malfunction or failure to conform with the written warranty , including the items or services the warrantor will pay for or provide, and, where necessary for clarification, those which the warrantor will not pay for or provide; (4) The point in time or event on which the warranty term commences, if different from the purchase date, and the time period or other measurement of warranty duration; (5) A step-by-step explanation of the procedure which the consumer should follow in order to obtain performance of any warranty obligation, including the persons or class of persons authorized to perform warranty obligations. This includes the name(s) of the warrantor(s), together with: The mailing address(es) of the warrantor(s), and/or the name or title and the address of any employee or department of the warrantor responsible for the performance of warranty obligations, and/or a telephone number which consumers may use without charge to obtain information on warranty performance; (6) Information respecting the availability of any informal dispute settlement mechanism elected by the warrantor in compliance with part 703 of this subchapter; (7) Any limitations on the duration of implied warranties, disclosed on the face of the warranty as provided in section 108 of the Act, 15 U.S.C. 2308 , accompanied by
perturbation.justification: The modified text, which states that Metavante's liability will be determined at its 'sole discretion,' directly contradicts consumer protection laws. Specifically, it undermines the requirement for warrantors to remedy consumer products within a reasonable time, as described in 15 U.S. Code § 2304. The original text provided a limit on liability, while the modified text removes this limit and allows Metavante to potentially avoid liability altogether, thereby violating the federal minimum standards for warranties. The changed text allows for the possibility of no remedy being provided, thereby conflicting with the obligation to remedy a product within a reasonable time as stated in the first snippet.
